summary: This article explores the 11th-century nun Hildegard of Bingen's pioneering views on the intersection of science and faith. It highlights her beliefs that the natural world possesses inherent dignity, that studying nature points directly to God, and that humans have a divine mandate to steward the earth's resources rather than exploit them.

principles.statement: The natural world possesses inherent dignity and is worthy of study.
principles.rationale: Despite the fall of humanity, creation was originally made perfect and from love by God, giving it an inherent beauty and worth that should be explored.
principles.source_quote: Love was in eternity and brought forth, in the beginning of all holiness, all creatures without any admixture of evil.

principles.statement: Observing the natural world naturally points us toward God.
principles.rationale: The wonder and awe experienced when studying the intricacies of nature reveal a divine creator who is the rightful recipient of our praise.
principles.source_quote: The blowing wind, the mild, moist air, the exquisite greening of trees and grasses – in their beginning, in their ending, they give God their praise

principles.statement: Nature is a gift provided for human use, but it must be stewarded wisely.
principles.rationale: God placed all things on earth for humans to use and work with for survival, which requires careful tending rather than reckless pillaging.
principles.source_quote: All nature is at the disposal of humankind. We are to work with it. For without we cannot survive.

mental_models.name: Greening Power
mental_models.description: A lens for viewing the vitality, health, and life-giving force of the natural world. It is a delicate balance that humans must awaken within themselves to protect, as damaging the environment diminishes this power and leads to ecological chaos.
mental_models.example: She predicted that if humans failed to preserve the natural world, the 'greening power of life on earth' would be reduced, resulting in altered air, contradictory chills, and paradoxical warmth.

signature_quotes.text: Glance at the sun. See the moon and the stars. Gaze at the beauty of earth’s greenings. Now, think. What delight God gives to humankind with all these things. . . . All nature is at the disposal of humankind. We are to work with it. For without we cannot survive.
signature_quotes.context: On the relationship between humanity and the natural world.

signature_quotes.text: Love was in eternity and brought forth, in the beginning of all holiness, all creatures without any admixture of evil.
signature_quotes.context: Personifying love as the source of creation's inherent goodness.

signature_quotes.text: The blowing wind, the mild, moist air, the exquisite greening of trees and grasses – in their beginning, in their ending, they give God their praise
signature_quotes.context: On how the natural world glorifies its creator.

signature_quotes.text: ...the greening power of life on earth (being) reduced in every seed because the upper region of the air was altered in a way contrary to its first destiny. Summer now became a subject to a contradictory chill, while winter often experienced paradoxical warmth.
signature_quotes.context: A prophetic warning about the consequences of failing to preserve the natural world.

anti_patterns.description: Viewing the natural world as totally corrupted and pointless to study.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores the 'original blessing' and inherent beauty of creation, failing to recognize that natural things were given dignity by God.

anti_patterns.description: Recklessly pillaging the earth of its natural resources.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It injures the earth and reduces the 'greening power of life', leading to catastrophic climate alterations like droughts, dampness, and paradoxical temperatures.
